comment_text.sn19.16:1.5: He would, obviously, decapitate his victims. For his name hārika, compare the suicide weapon (satthahāraka) which according to the commentary is a “taker of life” MN 145:5.32.

root_text.sn19.16:1.1: “Idhāhaṁ, āvuso, gijjhakūṭā pabbatā orohanto addasaṁ asīsakaṁ kabandhaṁ vehāsaṁ gacchantaṁ.
root_text.sn19.16:1.2: Tassa ure akkhīni ceva honti mukhañca.
root_text.sn19.16:1.3: Tamenaṁ gijjhāpi kākāpi kulalāpi anupatitvā anupatitvā vitacchenti virājenti.
root_text.sn19.16:1.5: eso, bhikkhave, satto imasmiṁyeva rājagahe hāriko nāma coraghātako ahosi …pe….

translation_text.sn19.16:1.1: “Just now, reverend, as I was descending from Vulture’s Peak Mountain I saw a headless trunk flying through the air.
translation_text.sn19.16:1.2: Its eyes and mouth were on its chest.
translation_text.sn19.16:1.3: Vultures, crows, and hawks kept chasing it, pecking and clawing
translation_text.sn19.16:1.5: “That being used to be an executioner named Taker right here in Rājagaha. …”

variant_text.sn19.16:1.1: asīsakaṁ kabandhaṁ → asīsakaṁ kavandhaṁ (bj, pts1ed, pts2ed); asīsakabandhaṁ (sya-all)
